question.p:
Question 1 of 782 : Microbiology
Are endotoxins heat stable? ... yes, stable at 100C for 1 hr.

Question 2 of 782 : Microbiology
Are endotoxins secreted from cells? ... NO

Question 3 of 782 : Microbiology
Are endotoxins used as antigens in vaccines? ... no, they don't produce protective immune response

Question 4 of 782 : Microbiology
Are exotoxins heat stable? ... no, destroyed rapidly at 60C (exception: Staphylococcal enterotoxin)

Question 5 of 782 : Microbiology
Are exotoxins secreted from cells? ... YES

Question 6 of 782 : Microbiology
Are exotoxins used as antigens in vaccines? ... Yes, TOXOIDS are used as vaccines

Question 7 of 782 : Microbiology
Describe the chemical composition of peptidoglycan. ... Sugar backbone with cross-linked peptide side chains.

Question 8 of 782 : Microbiology
Describe the major components of a G- cell wall. ... inner and outer lipid bilayer membranes - thin layer of peptidoglycan - periplasmic space - contains lipopolysaccharide, lipoprotein and phospholipid

Question 9 of 782 : Microbiology
Describe the major components of a G+ cell wall. ... one lipid bilayer membrane - thick layer of peptidoglycan - contains teichoic acid

Question 10 of 782 : Microbiology
Describe the process of conjugation. ... DNA transfer from one bacterium to another.

Question 11 of 782 : Microbiology
Describe the process of transduction. ... DNA transfer by a virus from one cell to another.

Question 12 of 782 : Microbiology
Describe the process of transformation. ... purified DNA is taken up by a cell

Question 13 of 782 : Microbiology
Does endotoxin induce an antigenic response? ... no, not well

Question 14 of 782 : Microbiology
Does exotoxin induce an antigenic response? ... Yes, induces high-titer antibodies called antitoxins

Question 15 of 782 : Microbiology
Give two general functions of peptidoglycan ... Gives rigid support - protects against osmotic pressure

Question 16 of 782 : Microbiology
How are Group A and Group B Strep primarily differentiated? ... Group A are Bacitracin sensitive - Group B are Bacitracin resistant

Question 17 of 782 : Microbiology
How are the pathogenic Neisseria species differentiated? ... on the basis of sugar fermentation

Question 18 of 782 : Microbiology
How are the species of Streptococcus primarily differentiated? ... on the basis of their HEMOLYTIC capabilities

Question 19 of 782 : Microbiology
List the four phases of the bacterial growth curve. ... Lag phase - log (exponential) phase - stationary phase - death phase

Question 20 of 782 : Microbiology
Name 2 G- rods that are considered slow lactose fermenters. ... Citrobacter and Serratia

Question 21 of 782 : Microbiology
Name 3 G- rods that are considered fast lactose fermenters. ... 1) Klebsiella 2) E. coli 3) Enterobacter

Question 22 of 782 : Microbiology
Name 3 G- rods which are lactose nonfermenters and Oxidase(-)? ... Shigella, Salmonella, Proteus

Question 23 of 782 : Microbiology
Name 4 bacteria that use IgA protease to colonize mucosal surfaces. ... 1) Strep. pneumoniae 2) Neisseria meningitidis 3) Neisseria gonorrhea 4) H. flu

Question 24 of 782 : Microbiology
Name 4 genus of bacteria that are G- 'coccoid' rods. ... 1) H. flu 2) Pasteruella 3) Brucella 4) Bordetella pertussis

Question 25 of 782 : Microbiology
Name 6 bacteria that don't Gram's stain well? ... Treponema - Rickettsia - Mycobacteria - Mycoplasma - Legionella pneumophila - Chlamydia

Question 26 of 782 : Microbiology
Name four genus of bacteria that are G+ rods. ... 1) Clostridium (an anaerobe) 2) Coynebacterium 3) Listeria 4) Bacillus

Question 27 of 782 : Microbiology
Name seven G+ bacteria species that make exotoxins. ... 1) Corynebacterium diphtheriae 2) Clostridium tetani 3) Clostridium botulinum 4) Clostridium perfringens 5) Bacillus anthracis 6) Staph. aureus 7) Strep. pyogenes

Question 28 of 782 : Microbiology
Name three diseases caused by exotoxins. ... Tetanus - botulism - diptheria

Question 29 of 782 : Microbiology
Name three G- bacteria species that make exotoxins. ... 1) E. coli 2) Vibrio cholerae 3) Bordetella pertussis

Question 30 of 782 : Microbiology
Name three Lactose-fermenting enterics. ... Eschericia, Klebsiella, Enterobacter

Question 31 of 782 : Microbiology
Name two diseases caused by endotoxins. ... Meningococcemia - sepsis by G(-) rods

Question 32 of 782 : Microbiology
Name two type of Strep that exhibit alpha hemolysis? ... S. pneumoniae - Viridans strep. (e.g. S. mutans)

Question 33 of 782 : Microbiology
Name two types of Strep. that are non-hemolytic (gamma hemolysis). ... Enterococcus (E. faecalis) and Peptostreptococcus (anaerobe)

Question 34 of 782 : Microbiology
Name two types of Strep. that exhibit beta hemolysis. ... Group A Strep. (GAS) and Group B Strep. (GBS)

Question 35 of 782 : Microbiology
Teichoic acid induces what two cytokines? ... TNF and IL-1

Question 36 of 782 : Microbiology
What are the effects of erythrogenic toxin? ... it is a superantigen - it causes rash of Scarlet fever

Question 37 of 782 : Microbiology
What are the effects of streptolysin O? ... it is a hemolysin - it is the antigen for ASO-antibody found in rheumatic fever

Question 38 of 782 : Microbiology
What are the effects of the exotoxin secreted by Bacillus anthracis? (1) ... one toxin in the toxin complex is an adenylate cyclase

Question 39 of 782 : Microbiology
What are the effects of the exotoxin secreted by Bordetella pertussis? (3) ... Stimulates adenylate cyclase by ADP ribosylation - causes whooping cough - inhibits chemokine receptor, causing lymphocytosis

Question 40 of 782 : Microbiology
What are the effects of the exotoxin secreted by Clostridium botulinum? ... blocks release of acetylcholine: causes anticholenergic symptoms, CNS paralysis; can cause 'floppy baby'

Question 41 of 782 : Microbiology
What are the effects of the exotoxin secreted by Clostridium perfringens? ... alpha toxin is a lecithinase - causes gas gangrene - get a double zone of hemolysis on blood agar

Question 42 of 782 : Microbiology
What are the effects of the exotoxin secreted by Clostridium tetani? ... blocks release of the inhibitory NT glycine; causes 'lockjaw'

Question 43 of 782 : Microbiology
What are the effects of the exotoxin secreted by Corynebacterium diphtheria? (3) ... 1) inactivates EF-2 by ADP ribosylation 2) pharyngitis 3) 'pseudomembrane' in throat

Question 44 of 782 : Microbiology
What are the effects of the exotoxin secreted by E. coli? (2) ... this heat labile toxin stimulates adenylate cyclase by ADP ribosylation of G protein - causes watery diarrhea

Question 45 of 782 : Microbiology
What are the effects of the exotoxin secreted by Staph. aureus? ... superantigen; induces IL-1 and IL-2 synthesis in Toxic Shock Syndrome; also causes food poisoning

Question 46 of 782 : Microbiology
What are the effects of the exotoxin secreted by Vibro cholerae? (3) ... Stimulates adenylate cyclase by ADP ribosylation of G protein - increases pumping of Cl- and H2O into gut - causes voluminous rice-water diarrhea

Question 47 of 782 : Microbiology
What are the general clinical effects of endotoxin?(2) ... fever, shock

Question 48 of 782 : Microbiology
What are three primary/general effects of endotoxin (especially lipid A)? ... 1) Acivates macrophages 2) Activates completment (alt. pathway) 3) Activates Hageman factor

Question 49 of 782 : Microbiology
What are two exotoxins secreted by Strep. pyogenes? ... Erythrogenic toxin and streptolysin O

Question 50 of 782 : Microbiology
What are two functions of the pilus/fimbrae? ... Mediate adherence of bacteria to the cell surface - sex pilus forms attachment b/t 2 bacteria during conjugation

Question 51 of 782 : Microbiology
(T/F) The outer membrane for G+ and the cell membrane for G- act as major surface antigens. ... TRUE: they DO act a major surface antigens

Question 52 of 782 : Microbiology
What are two species of Gram (-) cocci and how are they differentiated? ... 1) Neisseria memingitidis: maltose fermenter 2) Neisseria gonorrhoeae: maltose NONfementer

Question 53 of 782 : Microbiology
What bacteria produces a blue-green pigment? ... Pseudomonas aeruginosa

Question 54 of 782 : Microbiology
What bacteria produces a red pigment? ... Serratia marcescens ('maraschino cherries are red')

Question 55 of 782 : Microbiology
What bacteria produces a yellow pigment? ... Staph. aureus (Aureus= gold in Latin)

Question 56 of 782 : Microbiology
What culture requirements do Fungi have? ... Sabouraud's agar

Question 57 of 782 : Microbiology
What culture requirements do Lactose-fermenting enterics have? ... MacConkey's agar (make pink colonies)

Question 58 of 782 : Microbiology
What culture requirements does B. pertussis have? ... Bordet-Gengou (potato) agar

Question 59 of 782 : Microbiology
What culture requirements does C. diphtheriae have? ... Tellurite agar

Question 60 of 782 : Microbiology
What culture requirements does H. flu have? ... chocolate agar with factors V (NAD) and X (hematin)

Question 61 of 782 : Microbiology
What culture requirements does Legionella pneumophia have? ... Charcol yeast extract agar buffered with increased iron and cysteine

Question 62 of 782 : Microbiology
What culture requirements does N. gonorrhea have? ... Thayer-Martin (VCN) media

Question 63 of 782 : Microbiology
What G- rod is a lactose nonfermenter and is Oxidase+ ? ... Pseudomonas

Question 64 of 782 : Microbiology
What is a function of the plasma membrane in bacterial cells? ... site of oxidative and transport enzymes

Question 65 of 782 : Microbiology
What is a toxoid? ... exotoxin treated with formaldehyde (or acid or heat); retains antigeniciy but looses toxicity

Question 66 of 782 : Microbiology
What is an acronym for remembering 6 bacteria that don't Gram's stain well? ... TRMMLC: These Rascals May Microscopically Lack Color

Question 67 of 782 : Microbiology
What is meant by alpha, beta, and gamma hemolysis? ... On a Blood agar plate: alpha= partial, green; beta= clear; gamma= no hemolysis; red

Question 68 of 782 : Microbiology
What is the chemical composition of a glycocalyx? ... polysaccharide

Question 69 of 782 : Microbiology
What is the chemical composition of bacterial ribosomes? ... RNA and protein in 30S and 50S subunits

Question 70 of 782 : Microbiology
What is the chemical composition of endotoxin? ... Lipopolysaccharide

Question 71 of 782 : Microbiology
What is the chemical composition of exotoxin? ... polypeptide

Question 72 of 782 : Microbiology
What is the chemical composition of spores? ... keratin-like coat - dipicolinic acid

Question 73 of 782 : Microbiology
What is the funciton and chemical composition of the flagellum? ... for motility - made of protein

Question 74 of 782 : Microbiology
What is the function of a glycocalyx? ... mediates adherence to surfaces, especially foreign surfaces (i.e. catheters)

Question 75 of 782 : Microbiology
What is the function of spores? ... provides resistance to dehydration, heat, and chemicals

Question 76 of 782 : Microbiology
What is the major chemical composition of the capsule? ... Polysaccharide (*except Bacillus anthracis, which contains D-Glutamate)

Question 77 of 782 : Microbiology
What is the major function of the capsule? ... antiphagocytic

Question 78 of 782 : Microbiology
What is the mode of action of endotoxin? ... includes TNF and IL-1

Question 79 of 782 : Microbiology
What is the nature of the DNA transferred in conjugation? ... Chromosomal or plasmid

Question 80 of 782 : Microbiology
What is the nature of the DNA transferred in transduction? ... Any gene in generalized transduction; only certain genes in specialized transduction

Question 81 of 782 : Microbiology
What is the nature of the DNA transferred in transformation? ... Any DNA

Question 82 of 782 : Microbiology
What is the periplasm? Where is it found? ... the space between the inner and outer cell membranes found in G(-) bacteria.

Question 83 of 782 : Microbiology
What is the source of endotoxins? ... cell wall of most G- bacteria (think N-dotoxin=gram Negative)

Question 84 of 782 : Microbiology
What is the source of exotoxins? ... certain species of some G+ and G- bacteria

Question 85 of 782 : Microbiology
What is the unique chemical component of Gram (-) cell membranes? ... Lipopolysaccharide

Question 86 of 782 : Microbiology
What is the unique chemical component of Gram + cell membranes? ... Teichoic acid

Question 87 of 782 : Microbiology
What is used to stain Legionella? ... Use silver stain.

Question 88 of 782 : Microbiology
What species is Group A Strep? ... S. pyogenes

Question 89 of 782 : Microbiology
What species is Group B Strep? ... S. agalactiae

Question 90 of 782 : Microbiology
What stain is amyloid and gives an apple-green birefringence in polarized light? ... Congo red

Question 91 of 782 : Microbiology
What stain is used for acid fast bacteria? ... Ziehl-Neelsen

Question 92 of 782 : Microbiology
What stain is used for Borrelia, Plasmodium, trypanosomes, and Chlamydia? ... Giemsa's

Question 93 of 782 : Microbiology
What stain is used for Cryptococcus neoformans? ... India ink

Question 94 of 782 : Microbiology
What stains gylcogen, mucopolysaccharides and is used to diagnose Whipple's disease? ... PAS (periodic acid Schiff)

Question 95 of 782 : Microbiology
What test distinguishes Staph. and Strep? ... Staph. are Catalase (+) and are in clusters - Strep. are Catalase (-) and are in chains

Question 96 of 782 : Microbiology
What test distinguishes Staph. aureus from Staph. epidermidis and Staph. saprophyticus? ... S. aureus is Coagulase (+) - S. epidermidis and S. saprophyticus are Coagulase (-)

Question 97 of 782 : Microbiology
What two things distinguish S. pneumoniae from Viridans Strep.? ... S. pneumoniae: have Capsule; Optochin Sensitive - Viridans strep: No capsule; Optochin Resistant

Question 98 of 782 : Microbiology
What type of enzymes allows certain bacteria to colonize mucosal surfaces? ... IgA proteases

Question 99 of 782 : Microbiology
When endotoxin activates complement, what are the secondary effects? ... C3a: hypotension, edema; C5a: neutrophil chemotaxis

Question 100 of 782 : Microbiology
When endotoxin activates Hageman, what are the secondary effects? ... coagulation cascade: DIC

Question 101 of 782 : Microbiology
When endotoxin activates macrophages, what 3 cytokines are released and what are the secondary effects? ... IL-1--fever - TNF--fever, hemmoragic tissue necrosis - Nitic oxide--hypotension, shock

Question 102 of 782 : Microbiology
Where are the genes for endotoxin located? ... on the bacterial chromosome

Question 103 of 782 : Microbiology
Where are the genes for exotoxin located? ... on a plasmid or in a bacteriophage

Question 104 of 782 : Microbiology
Where are the spores of Clostridium botulinum found? ... canned food - honey

Question 105 of 782 : Microbiology
Where is LPS found? ... in the outer membrane of G (-) cell walls

Question 106 of 782 : Microbiology
Which has a higher toxicity: exotoxin or endotoxin? ... EXOTOXIN: fatal dose is ~1ug! (for endotoxin, fatal dose is hundreds of micrograms)

Question 107 of 782 : Microbiology
Which type of Neisseria ferment Glucose only? ... Gonococci (Glucose= Gonococci)

Question 108 of 782 : Microbiology
Which type of Neisseria ferment maltose and glucose? ... Meningococci (MaltoseGlucose= MeninGococci)

Question 109 of 782 : Microbiology
Which types of transfer can eukaryotic cells do? ... only transformation

Question 110 of 782 : Microbiology
Which types of transfer can prokaryotic cells do? ... all 3: conjugation, transduction, and transformation

Question 111 of 782 : Microbiology
Why don't Mycobacteria Gram's stain well? ... high lipid content cell wall requires acid-fast stain

Question 112 of 782 : Microbiology
Why don't Mycoplasma Gram's stain well? ... no cell wall

Question 113 of 782 : Microbiology
Why don't Rickettsia, Chlamydia, and Legionella Gram's stain well? ... they are intracellular (Legionella is Mainly intracellular)

Question 114 of 782 : Microbiology
Why don't Treponema Gram's stain well? ... too thin to be visualized (use darkfield microscopy and antibody staining)

Question 115 of 782 : Microbiology
Name 5 species of bacteria that are transmitted to humans from animals.(Acronym: BBugs From Your Pet.) ... Borrelia burgdorferi - Brucella spp. - Francisella tularensis - Yersinia pestis - Pasteurella multocida

Question 116 of 782 : Microbiology
All Rickettsiae (except one genus) are transmitted by what type of vector? ... arthropod (Coxiella is atypical: transmitted by aeresol)

Question 117 of 782 : Microbiology
Are G(-) bugs resistant to Pen G? to ampicillin? to vancomycin? ... G- bugs are resistant to PenG but may be susceptible to pen. derivative like ampicillin. The G- outer mb inhibits entry of PenG and vancomycin.

Question 118 of 782 : Microbiology
Are Strep. pneumoniae sensitve to optochin? Are Viridans strep.? ... Strep. pneumoniae is optochin-Sensitive - Viridans streptococci is optochin-Resistant

Question 119 of 782 : Microbiology
Is Strep. pyogenes Bacitracin-sensitive? ... Yes.

Question 120 of 782 : Microbiology
Are Viridans strep. alpha, beta, or non-hemolytic? ... alpha

Question 121 of 782 : Microbiology
Because of drug resistance, what in an alternate treatment combination for leprosy? ... rifampin with dapsone and clofazimine

Question 122 of 782 : Microbiology
Besides the rash, what other body systems are affected by Lyme disease? (3) ... joints -CNS -heart

Question 123 of 782 : Microbiology
Describe lab-findings for Pseudomonas aeruginosa. ... Aerobic, G(-) rod. - Non-lactose fermenting - Oxidase positive - Produces pyocyanin (blue-green pigment)

Question 124 of 782 : Microbiology
Describe the disease associated with M. avium-intracellulare. ... often resistant to multiple drugs; causes disseminated disease in AIDS.

Question 125 of 782 : Microbiology
Describe the H. flu vaccine. When is it given? ... contains type b capsular polysaccharide conjugated to diphtheria toxoid or other protein. -Given b/t 2m and 18m.

Question 126 of 782 : Microbiology
Describe the typical findings with diarrhea caused by enterotoxigenic E. coli. (3) ... 1) Ferments lactose 2) watery diarrhea 3) no fever/leukocytosis

Question 127 of 782 : Microbiology
Describe the typical findings with Vibro cholerae. (3) ... 1) Comma-shaped organisms 2) rice-water stools 3) no fever/leukocytosis

Question 128 of 782 : Microbiology
Do Streptococcus pneumonia have catalase? Do Viridans Strep. have catalase? ... NO. both are catalase -ve

Question 129 of 782 : Microbiology
Enterococci are hardier than nonenterococcal group D bacteria. What lab conditions can they grow in? ... 6.5% NaCl (used as lab test)

Question 130 of 782 : Microbiology
Following primary infection with TB, if preallergic lymphatic or hematogenous dissemination occurs, what follows? ... -dormant tubercle bacilli form in several organs - REACTIVATION can occur in adult life

Question 131 of 782 : Microbiology
Following primary infection with TB, if severe bacteremia occurs, what follows? ... Miliary tuberculosis and possibly death

Question 132 of 782 : Microbiology
Following primary infection with TB, if the lesion heals by fibrosis, what is the result? ... Immunity and hypersensitivity---> tuberculin positive

Question 133 of 782 : Microbiology
Following primary infection with TB, under what conditions would the lesion likely progress to lung disease? ... HIV, malnutrition. This progressive lung disease can rarely lead to death.

Question 134 of 782 : Microbiology
Following primary infection with TB, what are 4 possible courses the disease could take? ... 1) Heals by fibrosis 2) Progressive lung disease 3) Severe bacteremia 4) Preallergic lymphatic or hematogenous dissemination

Question 135 of 782 : Microbiology
Give 3 examples of obligate anaerobes. ... Clostridium - Bacteroides - Actinomyces

Question 136 of 782 : Microbiology
Give 3 types of infection Pseudomonas aeruginosa is commonly responsible for. ... 1) burn wound infection 2) nosocomial pneumonia 3) pneumonia with cystic fibrosis

Question 137 of 782 : Microbiology
Give 4 examples of encapsulated bacteria. ... 1) Strep. pneumoniae 2) Haemophilus influenza (especially b) 3) Neisseria memingitidis 4) Klebsiella pneumoniae

Question 138 of 782 : Microbiology
H. flu causes what? (4) ... Epiglottitis -Meningitis -Otitis media -Pneumonia (haEMOPhilus)

Question 139 of 782 : Microbiology
How are Borrelia visualized? ... using aniline dyes (Wright's or Giemsa stain) in light microscopy

Question 140 of 782 : Microbiology
How are Mycobacteria visualized in the lab? ... acid-fast stain =Ziehl-Neelson

Question 141 of 782 : Microbiology
How are Treponema visualized? ... by dark-field microscopy

Question 142 of 782 : Microbiology
How can secondary tuberculosis in the lung occur?(2) ... 1) Reinfection of partially immune hypersensitized hosts (usu. adults) =exogenous source 2) Reactivation of dormant tubercle bacilli in immunocompromised or debilitated hosts =endogenous source

Question 143 of 782 : Microbiology
How can you remember that Viridans strep are resistant to optochin? ... they live in the mouth and are not afraid of the (opto-)CHIN

Question 144 of 782 : Microbiology
How does primary syphilis present? ... with a painless chancre (localized disease; 2-10 wks).

Question 145 of 782 : Microbiology
How does secondary syphilis present? ... disseminated disease (1-3m later) with constitutional symptoms, maculopapular rash, condylomata lata (genital lesions)

Question 146 of 782 : Microbiology
How does tertiary syphilis present? ... gummas (granulomas), aortitis, neurosyphilis (tabes dorsalis), Argyll-Robertson pupil

Question 147 of 782 : Microbiology
How does the bacterium C. diphtheria cause disease? ... via exotoxin encoded by beta-prophage; exotoxin inhibits protein synthesis via ADP-ribosylation of EF-2

Question 148 of 782 : Microbiology
How does the rash with typhus differ from the rash with RMSF(Rocky Mountan Spotted Fever)? ... typhus: maculopapillary rash, BEGINS ON TRUNK, moves peripherally -RMSF (Rocky Mountan Spotted Fever): macules progressing to petichiae, BEGINS ON HANDS & FEET and moves inward.

Question 149 of 782 : Microbiology
How is Brucellosis/Undulant fever transmitted? ... dairy products, contact with animals

Question 150 of 782 : Microbiology
How is Cellulitis transmitted? ... Animal bite; cats, dogs (Pasteurella multocida)

Question 151 of 782 : Microbiology
How is H. flu transmitted? ... aeresol

Question 152 of 782 : Microbiology
How is Legionnaires' disease diagnosed in lab? ... use silver stain (doesn't Gram stain well) -culture with charcoal yeast extract with iron and cysteine.

Question 153 of 782 : Microbiology
How is Legionnaires' disease transmitted? ... aeresol transmission from envirnomental water source habitat (NO human-to-human transmission).

Question 154 of 782 : Microbiology
How is Lyme disease transmitted? ... Tick bite; Ixodes ticks that live of deer and mice

Question 155 of 782 : Microbiology
How is Shigella spread? ... food, fingers, feces, and flies

Question 156 of 782 : Microbiology
How is the Plague transmitted? ... Flea bite; rodents, especially prairie dogs

Question 157 of 782 : Microbiology
How is Tuleremia transmitted? ... Tick bite; rabbits, deer

Question 158 of 782 : Microbiology
Is Bacillus anthracis G+ or G-? What is its morphology? ... It is a G+, spore-forming rod

Question 159 of 782 : Microbiology
Is there an animal reservoir for leprosy? ... Yes, armadillos in the US

Question 160 of 782 : Microbiology
List 5 findings associated with rheumatic fever. (Hint: PECCS) ... Polyarthritis - Erythema marginatum -Chorea - Carditis - Subcutaneous nodules

Question 161 of 782 : Microbiology
List the 'ABCDEFG' of diphtheria. ... ADP ribosylation -Beta-prophage -Corynebacterium - Diphtheria - Elongation Factor 2 - Granules

Question 162 of 782 : Microbiology
Name 2 alpha-hemolytic bacteria. ... Strep. pneumoniae - Viridans streptococci

Question 163 of 782 : Microbiology
Name 2 bugs that cause diarrhea but NOT fever and leukocytosis? ... E. coli and Vibro cholerae

Question 164 of 782 : Microbiology
Name 2 disease processes that can be caused by enterococci. ... 1) UTI 2) subacute endocarditis

Question 165 of 782 : Microbiology
Name 2 species of enterococci. ... Enterococcus faecalis -Enterococcus faecium

Question 166 of 782 : Microbiology
Name 2 symptoms of diphtheria. ... pseudomembraneous pharyngitis (grayish white membrane) - lymphadenopathy

Question 167 of 782 : Microbiology
Name 3 spore forming bacteria. ... Bacillus anthracis - Clostridium perfringens - C. tetani

Question 168 of 782 : Microbiology
Name 4 beta-hemolytic bacteria. ... 1) Staph. aureus 2) Strep. pyogenes (GAS) 3) Strep. agalactiae (GBS) 4) Listeria monocytogenes

Question 169 of 782 : Microbiology
Name 4 lactose-fermenting enteric bacteria. ... Klebsiella -E. coli -Enterobacter -Citrobacter (think Lactose is KEE for first three listed)

Question 170 of 782 : Microbiology
Name 4 obligate aerobic bacteria. ... Norcardia - Pseudomonas aeruginosa - Mycobacterium tuberculosis - Bacillus

Question 171 of 782 : Microbiology
Name 5 bugs that cause watery diarrhea. ... 1) Vibrio cholerae 2) enterotoxigenic E. coli 3) viruses (rotavirus) 4) protozoa (Cryptosporidium and (5) Giardia)

Question 172 of 782 : Microbiology
Name 6 bugs that cause bloody diarrhea. ... 1) Salmonella 2) Shigella 3) Campylobacter jejuni 4) enterohemorrhagic/enteroinvasive E.coli 5) Yersinia enterocilitica 6) Entamoeba histolytica (a protozoan)

Question 173 of 782 : Microbiology
Name 7 facultative intracellular bacteria. ... 1) Mycobacterium 2) Brucella 3) Francisella 4) Listeria 5) Yersinia 6) Legionella 7) Salmonella

Question 174 of 782 : Microbiology
Name three genera of spirochetes. ... Borrelia (big size) - Leptospira -Treponema (think: BLT; B is big)

Question 175 of 782 : Microbiology
Name two lab tests used to detect syphilis? ... VDRL and FTA-ABS

Question 176 of 782 : Microbiology
Name two non-lactose fermenting bacteria that invade intestinal mucosa and can cause bloody diarrhea. ... Salmonella and Shigella

Question 177 of 782 : Microbiology
Name two obligate intracellular bacteria. ... Rickettsia and Chlamydia (Hint: 'stay inside when its Really Cold.')

Question 178 of 782 : Microbiology
RMSF is endemic to what part of the US? ... the East Coast (in spite of the name)

Question 179 of 782 : Microbiology
Spores are formed by certain species of what type of bacteria? ... Gram+ rods, usually in soil; form spores only when nutrients are limited

Question 180 of 782 : Microbiology
T/F Chlamydia are obligate intracellular parasites that cause mucosal infections. ... TRUE

Question 181 of 782 : Microbiology
T/F Chlamys means cloak. ... TRUE (intracellular)

Question 182 of 782 : Microbiology
T/F Enterobacteriaceae are oxidase negative and are glucose fermenters. ... TRUE

Question 183 of 782 : Microbiology
T/F H. pylori infection is a risk factor for peptic ulcer and gastric carcinoma. ... TRUE

Question 184 of 782 : Microbiology
T/F Penicillin is not an effective treatment against Mycoplasma pneumoniae. ... TRUE Mycoplama are naturally resistant b/c they have no cell wall.

Question 185 of 782 : Microbiology
T/F Pseudomonas produces both endotoxin and exotoxin. ... TRUE: endotoxin---> fever, shock -exotoxin---> inactivates EF-2

Question 186 of 782 : Microbiology
T/F Rickettsiae are obligate intracellular parasites and need CoA and NAD. ... TRUE

Question 187 of 782 : Microbiology
T/F Some enterococci are resistant to PenG. ... FALSE: ALL enterococci are naturally resistant to Pen/cephlosporins.

Question 188 of 782 : Microbiology
T/F Spores have no metabolic activity. ... TRUE

Question 189 of 782 : Microbiology
T/F: S. aureus food poisoning is due to the ingestion of bacteria that rapidly secrete toxin once they enter the GI tract. ... FALSE: rapid onset of S. aureus food poisoning is due to injestion of PREFORMED toxin

Question 190 of 782 : Microbiology
The Weil-Felix reaction usually tests positive for what two diseases? Negative for what? Cross reacts with what? ... Positive: typhus and RMSF -Negative: Q fever -Cross-reacts: with Proteus antigen

Question 191 of 782 : Microbiology
Think COFFEe for Enterobacteriaceae. What does that stand for? ... Capsular -O-antigen -Flagellar antigen -Ferment glucose -Enterobacteriaceae

Question 192 of 782 : Microbiology
What's a pneumonic for remembering 4 obligate aerobes? ... Nagging Pests Must Breath (=Norcardia - Pseudomonas aeruginosa - Mycobacterium tuberculosis - Bacillus

Question 193 of 782 : Microbiology
What (6) infections can Pseudomonas aeruginosa cause? ... burn-wound infections -Pneumonia (esp. in cystic fibrosis) -Sepsis (black skin lesions) -External Otitis (swimmer's ear) - UTI -hot tub folliculitis

Question 194 of 782 : Microbiology
What 2 bugs can cause bloody diarrhea, fever, and leukocytosis, but do not ferment lactose? ... Salmonella and Shigella

Question 195 of 782 : Microbiology
What animals carry Lyme disease? ... The Ixodes tick transmits it. - Deer are required for tick life cycle. - Mice are important reservoirs.

Question 196 of 782 : Microbiology
What anitbody class is necessary for an immune response to encapsulated bacteria? ... IgG2.

Question 197 of 782 : Microbiology
What are 2 disease processes caused by Viridans strep and what species are responsible? ... 1) dental caries: Strep. mutans 2) bacterial endocarditis: Strep. sanguis

Question 198 of 782 : Microbiology
What are 3 options for triple therapy treatment of H. pylori? ... (1) bismuth (Pepto-Bismal), metronidazole, and tetracyclin or amoxicillin. OR (2) metronidazole, omeprazole, and clarithromycin (#2 is more expensive) OR (3) amoxicillin, omeprazole, and clarithromycin.

Question 199 of 782 : Microbiology
What are 3 advantages/differences between VDRL and FTA-ABS? ... FTA-ABS is 1) more specific 2) positive earlier in disease 3) remains positive longer than VDRL

Question 200 of 782 : Microbiology
